name: Scott Joplin
abstract: Scott Joplin (November 24, 1868 – April 1, 1917) was an American composer and pianist. Dubbed the "King of Ragtime", he composed more than 40 ragtime pieces, one ragtime ballet (original version of "The Ragtime Dance", 1899/1902), and two operas.

works_created.t: Rag-time dance: a stop-time two step

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Carew
about_quotes.evidence: They say that Joplin wasn't a very good player... But I heard a party that lived in Memphis... that told me that if they could get Scott Joplin for their parties to do the play, they didn't want anything else.

about_quotes.speaker: Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Scott Joplin, whose the king of ragtime, was a classical, he studied classical music. His music is classical, ragtime music was classical, and Scott Joplin was the best there was, he was the best there was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gerald Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses Scott Joplin's music, history, and influence on jazz in an academic/historical context, but there is no evidence of a personal relationship, meeting, or direct collaboration.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: In the old days, Scott Joplin used to bring many of his scores down to let my father's orchestra try them out for him... My mother used to play all of the Maple Leaf Rags and things of Scott.

about_quotes.speaker: Herman Autrey
about_quotes.evidence: A lot of people had never heard of him, and never heard of this, but by golly, look, the whole -- it came back. His tunes came back and upset the whole country.

about_quotes.speaker: Chuck Israels
about_quotes.evidence: I’m talking about the period starting with Scott Joplin and Jelly Roll Morton and John Philip Sousa, that’s the late nineteenth century
about_quotes.doc_title: Chuck Israels interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 11, 2021
